title: Composition
narrativeIntro: Everything you've learned this week works in isolation. But real systems compose — a component that uses a service that makes HTTP calls, displayed through a template with routing and parameters. Today you compose. Today the individual skills become a coherent whole.
narrativeOutro: This is what it feels like. Not individual skills, but orchestration. Components, services, HTTP, routing — all working in concert. The Drift's third cluster blazes with interconnected light.
phmConnectionNote: Ryland combines everything he's learned into more sophisticated solutions. You build complex Angular features.
conceptsPreview: Composing components, services, and HTTP into a complete feature

challenges.title: Data Flow
challenges.narrativeHook: Component calls service. Service filters data. Result flows back. What does the component display?
challenges.instructions:
Trace the data flow through three layers: the service filters a dataset, `ngOnInit` stores the result, and `count` reads from it.

Predict what `c.count` returns after `ngOnInit()` runs.
challenges.solution:
// No code changes — this is a prediction challenge.
// Answer: 2 — DataService.getActive() filters to Alpha and Gamma (active: true), so signals.length is 2.
challenges.predictPrompt: What does c.count return after ngOnInit() runs?

explainGate.prompt: Trace the data flow step by step: what does ngOnInit() do, what does DataService.getActive() return, and why is count 2 and not 3?

hints: Start at ngOnInit(): it calls this.service.getActive() and stores the result in this.signals.
getActive() filters the signals array — only entries where active === true pass through.
Alpha and Gamma are active: true. Beta is active: false. So getActive() returns 2 items, and count is 2.

challenges.title: Search Filter
challenges.narrativeHook: The catalog is too large to scroll. A search box filters it in real time.
challenges.instructions:
Complete the `CatalogComponent`:

- `searchTerm` holds the current input value (starts as `''`)
- `filteredItems` getter returns all items whose `name` contains `searchTerm` (case-insensitive)
- If `searchTerm` is empty, return all items

The `items` array is already populated.

files.name: catalog.component.ts

tests.description: filteredItems with empty searchTerm returns all 5 items
tests.testCode: const c = new CatalogComponent(); if (c.filteredItems.length !== 5) throw new Error(`Empty search should return 5 items, got ${c.filteredItems.length}`);
const c = new CatalogComponent(); c.searchTerm = 'alpha'; if (c.filteredItems.length !== 2) throw new Error(`'alpha' should match 2 items, got ${c.filteredItems.length}`);
const c = new CatalogComponent(); c.searchTerm = 'BETA'; if (c.filteredItems.length !== 1) throw new Error(`'BETA' should match 1 item, got ${c.filteredItems.length}`);
const c = new CatalogComponent(); c.searchTerm = 'xyz'; if (c.filteredItems.length !== 0) throw new Error(`'xyz' should match 0 items, got ${c.filteredItems.length}`);

hints: If searchTerm is empty/falsy, return this.items directly.
Use .toLowerCase() on both the search term and the item name for case-insensitive matching.

challenges.title: Debounce Input
challenges.narrativeHook: The search fires on every keystroke. The service can't keep up. Slow it down.
challenges.instructions:
Complete the `onInput` method of `SearchComponent` so it debounces search requests:

- Update `this.query` immediately with the new term
- Cancel any pending timer (`this.timerId`) using `clearTimeout`
- Schedule `this.searchService.search(term)` to run after **300ms** using `setTimeout`
- Store the result in `this.results` and reset `this.timerId` to `null`

If the user types again before 300ms passes, the previous timer is cancelled and a new one starts. Only the final pause triggers the search.

files.name: search.component.ts

tests.testCode: const mockService = { search: (t) => [t + '-result'] }; const c = new SearchComponent(mockService); c.onInput('alpha'); if (c.query !== 'alpha') throw new Error(`query should be 'alpha' immediately, got '${c.query}'`);

tests.description: results remain empty before the debounce timer fires
tests.testCode: const mockService = { search: (t) => [t + '-result'] }; const c = new SearchComponent(mockService); c.onInput('alpha'); if (c.results.length !== 0) throw new Error(`results should be empty before debounce fires, got ${c.results.length}`);

tests.description: results are populated after the debounce delay
tests.testCode: const mockService = { search: (t) => [t + '-result'] }; const c = new SearchComponent(mockService); c.onInput('alpha'); await new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, 350)); if (c.results.length !== 1 || c.results[0] !== 'alpha-result') throw new Error(`results should contain 'alpha-result' after debounce, got ${JSON.stringify(c.results)}`);

tests.description: rapid inputs cancel previous timers — search is called only once
tests.testCode: let callCount = 0; const mockService = { search: (t) => { callCount++; return [t + '-result']; } }; const c = new SearchComponent(mockService); c.onInput('a'); c.onInput('al'); c.onInput('alp'); await new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, 350)); if (callCount !== 1) throw new Error(`search should be called once after rapid inputs, got ${callCount} calls`);

explainGate.prompt: Explain what debouncing does and why it matters here. What happens to the previous timer when the user types a second character before 300ms passes?

hints: First check if this.timerId is not null and call clearTimeout(this.timerId) to cancel the previous scheduled search.
Then use setTimeout(() => { ... }, 300) to schedule the search. Store the return value in this.timerId.
Inside the setTimeout callback: this.results = this.searchService.search(term); this.timerId = null;

challenges.title: Master-Detail
challenges.narrativeHook: A list on one side, a detail panel on the other. Selecting an item updates the view.
challenges.instructions:
Complete the `MasterDetailComponent`:

- `select(id)` sets `selectedId` to the given id
- `selectedItem` getter returns the item whose `id` matches `selectedId`, or `null`
- `detailLabel` getter returns `'Viewing: ' + item.name` if an item is selected, else `'Select an item'`

files.name: master-detail.component.ts

tests.description: selectedItem should be null before any selection
tests.testCode: const c = new MasterDetailComponent(); if (c.selectedItem !== null) throw new Error('selectedItem should be null initially');

tests.description: select(2) should set selectedItem to the matching item
tests.testCode: const c = new MasterDetailComponent(); c.select(2); if (!c.selectedItem || c.selectedItem.name !== 'Deep Probe Beta') throw new Error(`Expected 'Deep Probe Beta', got ${c.selectedItem?.name}`);

tests.description: detailLabel should say 'Select an item' before selection
tests.testCode: const c = new MasterDetailComponent(); if (c.detailLabel !== 'Select an item') throw new Error(`Expected 'Select an item', got '${c.detailLabel}'`);

tests.description: detailLabel should show item name after selection
tests.testCode: const c = new MasterDetailComponent(); c.select(1); if (c.detailLabel !== 'Viewing: Relay Station Alpha') throw new Error(`Expected 'Viewing: Relay Station Alpha', got '${c.detailLabel}'`);

hints: selectedItem: return this.items.find(i => i.id === this.selectedId) ?? null;
detailLabel: return this.selectedItem ? 'Viewing: ' + this.selectedItem.name : 'Select an item';
